content.content:
1	# Design a Distributed Messaging System (Kafka / RabbitMQ)
2	
3	A durable pub/sub log: producers publish to **topics**, consumers read by **offset**, data is **partitioned** and **replicated**, ordering is guaranteed **within a partition**, and delivery is **at-least-once** by default. I'm targeting an internal eventing backbone for a large product org — so I'll work backwards from the platform team that owns it, and I'll keep **cost and operations first-class** the whole way through, because at this scale the bill and the on-call pager are the design.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions
8	
9	> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock scope with a couple of quick assumptions, state the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and core entities, then draw the high-level design one flow at a time — produce, replicate, consume — and go deep on the two or three hardest parts: ordering and replication, consumer-group offsets and rebalancing, and back-pressure on slow consumers. I'll check in with you as I go. Sound good?"
10	
11	The core tension in a messaging system is this: **we want a durable, ordered, infinitely-replayable log AND high throughput AND it must never lose an acknowledged write AND a slow consumer must never take down a fast producer.** Those pull against each other, and where they collide is where the interesting design lives.
12	
13	### 🔒 Scope lock (stating these as vetoable assumptions, stop me if any is wrong)
14	
15	1. **It's a durable *log*, not a transient queue.** I'll assume Kafka-style semantics: messages are retained on disk for a window (say 7 days) and a consumer reads by **offset** (its position in the log) and can rewind. *This forks the whole design* — if you actually want RabbitMQ-style semantics (a broker that deletes a message once it's acknowledged, with per-message routing), the storage model and delivery story change completely. I'll flag that fork in §2.
16	2. **Ordering is per-partition, not global.** I'll assume we guarantee ordering only *within a partition* (a single append-only shard of a topic), not across the whole topic. Global ordering would force a single writer and kill throughput.
17	3. **At-least-once delivery is the default; exactly-once is opt-in.** I'll assume consumers must tolerate duplicates by default (idempotent processing), and we offer an exactly-once mode for the topics that pay for it.
18	4. **Single region, multi-AZ to start.** I'll assume one region spread across 3 availability zones (separate data-centre buildings) for durability; multi-region/geo-replication is a §2 fork, not the baseline.
19	
20	> 🤝 **In / out of scope — add or cut before I commit?** *In:* produce, replicate (leader/follower with ISR), consume via consumer groups with offset management and rebalancing, retention, back-pressure. *Out (below the line):* the exact wire protocol bytes, schema registry, stream processing (Flink/Kafka Streams), tiered cloud storage internals, and full multi-region active-active. I'll mention each where it bites.
21	
22	### Functional requirements (the core flows — derived from the locks above)
23	
24	Because we locked "durable log + per-partition ordering + at-least-once," the core flows are:
25	
26	**F1 — Produce: publish a record to a topic.** A producer sends a record (key + value) to a topic; the broker appends it durably and returns an offset.
27	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** an app says "here's an event," and the system promises "I've written it down and it won't be lost," then hands back a receipt number.
28	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** "durably written" means it's safely on *multiple* machines before we acknowledge — but waiting for every copy is slow, so we have to pick exactly how many copies count as "safe."
29	
30	**F2 — Replicate: keep N copies in sync with a leader.** Each partition has one **leader** and follower replicas; the leader is the only writer, followers pull and stay in sync.
31	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** every shard has a boss who takes all the writes and a couple of backups that copy the boss continuously, so if the boss dies a backup takes over without losing data.
32	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** "in sync" is fuzzy — a follower that's lagging is *almost* a copy. We need a precise rule for which followers count as safe (the **ISR**, in-sync replica set), and what happens at the edge when the set shrinks to one.
33	
34	**F3 — Consume: read by offset within a consumer group.** A consumer in a **consumer group** is assigned partitions and reads forward from its committed offset; it commits offsets to mark progress.
35	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** a reader gets handed a few of the shards, reads through them like pages in a book, and bookmarks the page it reached so it can resume after a restart.
36	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** when a consumer joins or dies, the partitions must be *reassigned* across the group (a **rebalance**) without two consumers reading the same partition at once and without losing the bookmark.
37	
38	**F4 — Manage retention & replay.** Old data ages out by time or size; consumers can reset their offset to replay.
39	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the log keeps a week of history then throws away the oldest pages; a reader can flip back to re-read anything still on disk.
40	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** deleting old data cheaply (without churning the whole log) and bounding storage cost is the dominant cost lever at this scale.
41	
42	#### Below the line (out of scope for the main pass)
43	- Schema registry / Avro evolution, stream processing operators, exact protocol framing, tiered storage to object store (I'll mention as a cost lever), full active-active multi-region, ACL/quota admin UI.
44	
45	### Non-Functional requirements (provisional targets, tagged to the assumption they lean on)
46	
47	**N1 — Durability: no acknowledged write is ever lost.** Target: **RPO ≈ 0** for `acks=all` writes (we lose zero acknowledged data on a single-AZ failure).
48	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** once we say "got it," that event survives a machine — even a whole building — going down.
49	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** a CDC (change-data-capture) topic that mirrors a database loses a row update, and now a downstream system silently disagrees with the source of truth forever.
50	
51	**N2 — Produce latency: p99 ≈ 10ms** for `acks=all` within region (ASSUMING single-region; relaxes if multi-region).
52	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** 99 out of 100 publishes finish in under ~10 milliseconds.
53	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** synchronous request paths that publish-then-respond (e.g. order placed → emit event) stall, and the user-facing API inherits broker latency.
54	
55	**N3 — Throughput & availability: sustain peak ingest with 99.95% broker availability.**
56	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the cluster keeps accepting writes essentially all the time — about 4 hours of disruption per year, total.
57	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** producers buffer in memory, then start dropping events when their buffer fills — a silent data-loss outage that looks fine until you check downstream.
58	
59	**N4 — Ordering: per-partition total order, never reordered.**
60	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** within one shard, events come out in exactly the order they went in.
61	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** an event sourcing consumer applies "account closed" before "account opened" and corrupts state.
62	
63	> 💬 "The whole game here is durability and ordering versus throughput and latency — I'll keep naming which one I'm trading whenever I make a call."
64	
65	> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** true exactly-once across produce→process→produce needs transactional/idempotent producers plus a coordinator — I'll cover at-least-once as the baseline and treat exactly-once as a costed deep dive, not free.
66	
67	> 🎙️ **Script:** "I'm treating this as a Kafka-style durable log — producers append to partitioned topics, consumers read by offset and can replay. Ordering is per-partition only; delivery is at-least-once by default with exactly-once as an opt-in. The non-negotiables are durability — RPO near zero on acknowledged writes — and per-partition ordering. Everything else, including latency, I'll trade against those when I have to. One region across three AZs to start; I'll call out where going multi-region or switching to RabbitMQ-style queueing would fork the design."
68	
69	---
70	
71	## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions (what changes if I'm wrong)
72	
73	These are the residual forks from the scope lock — the two places my design is most sensitive. I'm not re-asking scale; I committed to a trillion-messages-a-day log. These are the *shape* forks.
74	
75	**Fork A — Log (Kafka) vs Queue (RabbitMQ).** I locked "durable replayable log, read by offset."
76	- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
77	  - *If it's actually a queue (RabbitMQ/SQS-style)* → messages are deleted on ack, there's no offset or replay, the broker does per-message routing and tracks per-message delivery state, and back-pressure is naturally handled by the queue draining. The storage model becomes a mutable per-message store, not an append-only log.
78	  - *If it stays a log* → storage is append-only segments, consumers own their position, and replay is free — but back-pressure and "did everyone read it yet?" become *our* problem, not the broker's.
79	- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'm assuming a replayable log like Kafka — if you actually want point-to-point queueing where messages vanish once handled, tell me and I'll switch to a per-message-state broker, it's a real fork."
80	
81	**Fork B — At-least-once vs exactly-once as the *default*.** I locked at-least-once.
82	- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
83	  - *If exactly-once must be the default* → every producer needs an idempotent producer ID + sequence numbers, every multi-step consume-process-produce needs broker transactions and a transaction coordinator, and throughput drops and latency rises across the board.
84	  - *If at-least-once is fine* → consumers dedupe with an idempotency key, and we only pay the exactly-once tax on the few topics that need it.
85	- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "Default is at-least-once with idempotent consumers — if your downstream genuinely can't dedupe, I'll turn on transactional exactly-once for that topic and we'll eat the throughput cost."
86	
87	**Fork C — Single-region vs multi-region durability.** I locked single-region, multi-AZ.
88	- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
89	  - *If we need region-failure survival (RPO≈0 across regions)* → we need synchronous cross-region replication (latency tax on every `acks=all` write) or async mirroring (RPO = the mirror lag, seconds of potential loss).
90	  - *If multi-AZ is enough* → we survive a building failure with zero data loss and zero added write latency.
91	- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "Multi-AZ gets us building-failure survival for free; surviving a whole region means either paying cross-region write latency or accepting a few seconds of possible loss — which do you want?"
92	
93	> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my three forks — log-vs-queue, exactly-once default, and multi-region. Happy with Kafka-style log, at-least-once default, single-region-multi-AZ? If so I'll size it."
94	
95	---
96	
97	## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)
98	
99	I deferred the math until now; here's where a number actually drives a decision. I ran a capacity model (`capacity.py`) rather than hand-waving. Glossing terms inline: **QPS** = messages per second hitting the cluster; **RF** = replication factor, how many copies of each partition we keep; **ISR** = in-sync replicas; **cross-AZ** = network that crosses between data-centre buildings (it's billed per gigabyte).
100	
101	**Anchor assumptions:** ~1 trillion messages/day, ~1 KB average record, peak = 3× average (diurnal + retry storms), RF=3, ~3 consumer groups read each topic on average, 7-day retention.
102	
103	| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
104	|---|---|---|---|---|
105	| Avg produce rate | 1e12 ÷ 86,400s | ~11.6M/s | "about twelve million messages a second" | Need thousands of partitions; no single node ingests this |
106	| Peak produce rate | 11.6M × 3 | ~35M/s | "about thirty-five million a second at peak" | Sizing target for partitions + brokers |
107	| Peak ingest bandwidth | 35M/s × 1KB | ~35 GB/s | "thirty-five gigabytes a second in" | Drives NIC + broker count |
108	| **Peak cluster network** | 35 GB/s ingest + 2×35 (replication to followers) + 3×35 (consume fan-out) | **~208 GB/s** | "about two hundred GB/s flying around the cluster" | **Network is the throughput bottleneck, not CPU** |
109	| Brokers for network | 208 GB/s ÷ (60% of 25 Gbps ≈ 1.9 GB/s) | ~111 | "around a hundred brokers for the wire" | Lower bound on fleet |
110	| Partitions needed | 35 GB/s ÷ 10 MB/s per partition | ~3,500 | "roughly three-and-a-half thousand partitions" | Ordering/parallelism unit; metadata pressure |
111	| Storage footprint | 1 TB/day × 7d × RF 3 × 1.3 slack | ~27 PB | "around twenty-seven petabytes on disk" | Drives broker count UP past the network number |
112	| **Brokers for storage** | 27 PB ÷ 20 TB usable per broker | **~1,365** | "about fourteen hundred brokers" | **THIS dominates — we're storage-bound** |
113	| Cross-AZ network $ | avg replication + 70% of consume, crossing AZs, ×$0.02/GB | ~$2.5M/mo | "two and a half million a month just in cross-AZ traffic" | **Dominant cost driver** |
114	| Storage $ | 27 PB × $0.08/GB-mo | ~$2.2M/mo | "two-ish million in disk" | Retention is the cost lever |
115	| Total + unit econ | ~$6.7M/mo ÷ 30B msgs/1M | ~$0.22/1M | "about twenty-two cents per million messages" | The "is this worth it" number |
116	
117	**The ONE decision-forcing number:** we need **~111 brokers for the network but ~1,365 for storage — storage wins by ~12×.** So this is a **storage-bound** cluster, not a throughput-bound one. That single fact reorients the whole design: the cost lever isn't faster brokers, it's **retention**.
118	
119	**Flip threshold:** cutting retention from **7 days → 3 days frees ~57% of the fleet** (and proportional cross-AZ cost). If retention drops below where storage-brokers ≤ network-brokers (~roughly < 1 day at this rate), we flip back to being throughput-bound and would right-size on NICs instead. That's the threshold I'd watch.
120	
121	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Two numbers matter. One: we're at about twelve million messages a second average, thirty-five at peak — so this is thousands of partitions across hundreds of machines, full stop. Two, and this is the one that drives everything: I need about a hundred brokers to move the bytes, but about fourteen hundred to *store* seven days of them. So I'm storage-bound by twelve-to-one — which means my biggest cost lever is retention, not compute. Drop retention from seven days to three and I cut the fleet roughly in half. Storage and cross-AZ network are each about two-and-a-half million a month; that's where the bill lives."
122	
123	---
124	
125	## 4. Core Entities
126	
127	This is the first-draft scaffold — names and jobs. Full field definitions come in §7.
128	
129	- **Topic** — a named stream of records (e.g. `orders.v1`); owns its partition count, replication factor, and retention policy. It's the unit producers and consumers address.
130	  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** a numbered filing cabinet labelled "orders" — you don't put paper *in the cabinet*, you put it in one of its drawers.
131	  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "Can you change a topic's partition count later?" → You can *increase* it, but it breaks key-to-partition stickiness (a key's records may now land in a different partition), so ordering-by-key only holds for records written after the change.
132	
133	- **Partition** — one append-only, ordered shard of a topic; owns a monotonic sequence of records and the offset space. It is the unit of ordering, parallelism, and replication.
134	  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** a single drawer in the cabinet where pages are stacked in arrival order and numbered 0, 1, 2…; you can only add to the top.
135	  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "Why is the partition the ordering unit and not the topic?" → Global ordering needs a single writer; per-partition ordering lets us parallelize across thousands of independent writers while still guaranteeing order where it matters (same key → same partition).
136	
137	- **Replica (Leader / Follower + ISR)** — copies of a partition on different brokers; the **leader** takes all reads/writes, **followers** pull to stay in sync, and the **ISR** (in-sync replica set) is the subset caught up enough to count as durable.
138	  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** the leader is the official scribe; followers are clerks photocopying every page seconds behind; the ISR is "which clerks are current enough that we'd trust them to take over."
139	  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "What if the ISR shrinks to just the leader?" → With `min.insync.replicas=2`, the leader *refuses* `acks=all` writes (returns an error) rather than acknowledge data only one machine has — durability over availability for that write.
140	
141	- **Consumer Group** — a set of cooperating consumers that together read a topic; owns the partition→consumer assignment and the **committed offsets** (per-partition bookmarks).
142	  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** a reading club where the chapters (partitions) are split among members so no two read the same chapter, and the club keeps a shared bookmark per chapter.
143	  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "What happens when a consumer dies mid-read?" → A **rebalance** reassigns its partitions to surviving members, who resume from the last *committed* offset — anything processed-but-not-committed is re-delivered (that's the at-least-once duplicate window).
144	
145	- **Offset** — an integer position within a partition; the producer gets one back on write, the consumer commits one to mark progress.
146	  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** the page number; "I've read up to page 4,096."
147	  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "Where are committed offsets stored?" → In a special internal compacted topic (`__consumer_offsets`) — so offset commits are just more durable log writes, not a separate database to operate.
148	
149	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Five entities. Topic is the named stream; partition is the real workhorse — an append-only ordered shard, and it's the unit of ordering, parallelism, *and* replication all at once. Each partition has a leader plus follower replicas, and the in-sync set decides what counts as durable. On the read side, a consumer group splits partitions among its members and keeps a bookmark — an offset — per partition. The surprising one to most people is that offsets live in just another topic, so we don't run a separate database to track who read what. Full fields land in the data model."
150	
151	---
152	
153	## 5. API / Interface
154	
155	I'll define one interface per functional flow. These are the *control* and *data* plane operations; in practice clients use a binary protocol over long-lived TCP connections, but I'll express them REST-style for clarity, then note the protocol reality.
156	
157	**Cross-cutting (stated once):**
158	- **Versioning:** topics carry a name suffix (`orders.v1`); the *wire protocol* is versioned per-API-key with negotiation on connect, so old clients keep working (additive fields don't bump; breaking changes are a new protocol version). Topic schema versioning is the schema registry's job (out of scope).
159	- **Pagination / reads:** consumption is a **cursor by offset**, never offset/limit pagination — the consumer says "give me from offset X" and the broker streams forward. This is keyset-style by construction and stays O(1) regardless of how deep you are.
160	- **Security:** caller identity comes from **mTLS or SASL** (the connection is authenticated, never a client-supplied user id); **object-level authz** = per-topic ACLs check "is *this* principal allowed to PRODUCE/CONSUME *this* topic?" on every connection, not just "are they authenticated?"
161	
162	### F1 — Produce
163	
164	**`POST /v1/topics/{topic}/records`** — POST because it creates new durable state (appends a record); not idempotent by default.
165	- Key request fields:
166	  - `key: bytes` — the partition key; same key → same partition → ordering preserved for that key. *Why:* this is how ordering-where-it-matters is achieved.
167	  - `value: bytes` — the payload.
168	  - `acks: enum(0|1|all)` — durability level; `all` waits for the full ISR. *Why:* the producer chooses its own durability/latency trade-off per write.
169	  - `producerId + seq` (server-aware, set by idempotent producer) — *absent from a naive client*; enables dedupe so a retried produce doesn't double-append.
170	
171	```
172	// Request
173	POST /v1/topics/orders.v1/records
174	{ "key": "user-42", "value": "{...order placed...}", "acks": "all" }
175	
176	// Response 200
177	{ "topic": "orders.v1", "partition": 7, "offset": 8042316, "timestamp": "2026-06-21T10:00:00Z" }
178	```
179	- **Key decision — idempotent produce:** with `acks=all`, a network timeout makes the producer *retry*, which could append twice. The **idempotent producer** stamps each record with a `(producerId, partition, seq)`; the leader rejects a duplicate `seq`, so a retry is dedup'd server-side. `offset` is server-set — the client never picks it.
180	
181	### F2 — Replicate (internal, not a public API)
182	
183	**`FETCH (partition, fromOffset)`** — followers issue a fetch to the leader to pull new records; same primitive consumers use. *Why GET-like:* it's a pure read of the leader's log.
184	- Fields: `partition`, `fetchOffset` (the follower's high-water position), `maxBytes`.
185	- **Key decision:** replication *reuses the consume path* — a follower is just a special consumer reading from the leader. One mechanism, not two. The leader advances the **high-water mark** (the highest offset all ISR members have) only when the ISR has the data; consumers can only read up to the high-water mark, so they never see un-replicated records.
186	
187	### F3 — Consume + commit offset
188	
189	**`GET /v1/topics/{topic}/records?group={g}&maxBytes={n}`** — GET, pure read, streams records forward from the group's assigned partitions and committed offsets.
190	**`POST /v1/groups/{group}/offsets`** — POST, commits progress (creates new offset state).
191	- Fields: `group` (which consumer group), `partition`, `offset` (the next offset to read after restart). *Server-set:* the partition *assignment* is decided by the group coordinator, not the client — the client can't grab a partition another member owns.
192	
193	```
194	// Commit request
195	POST /v1/groups/billing-svc/offsets
196	{ "offsets": [ { "topic": "orders.v1", "partition": 7, "offset": 8042317 } ] }
197	
198	// Response 200
199	{ "committed": 1 }
200	```
201	- **Key decision — commit AFTER processing (at-least-once):** the consumer commits the offset only *after* it has durably processed the record. If it crashes between processing and committing, it reprocesses → duplicate. That's the at-least-once contract; the alternative (commit first) risks *losing* a message, which we refuse for durability.
202	
203	### F4 — Manage retention / replay
204	
205	**`PUT /v1/topics/{topic}/config`** — PUT, fully replaces the topic's retention config.
206	**`POST /v1/groups/{group}/offsets:reset`** — reset a group's offset to replay (`earliest`, `latest`, or a timestamp).
207	- **Key decision:** retention is enforced by **segment deletion** (drop whole files), and replay is just an offset reset — no data is copied, the consumer simply re-reads existing segments.
208	
209	> 💬 "The whole API is append, fetch, and commit-a-bookmark — that's it. Everything else is config."
210	
211	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Producers POST records with a key — the key decides the partition, which is how I keep ordering where it matters. The server hands back a partition and offset; the client never picks the offset, and an idempotent producer stamps a sequence number so a retry can't double-write. Consumers GET records forward by offset for their group, and POST a commit *after* they've processed — that's the at-least-once contract: a crash before commit means a re-read, never a loss. Replication quietly reuses the same fetch path — a follower is just a consumer reading the leader. Identity is mTLS, and per-topic ACLs check that this principal can touch this topic on every connection."
212

215	## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)
216	
217	I'll define the color language once and reuse it: blue = clients/producers/consumers, green = our broker services, amber = the log/async storage, grey = metadata + offset stores, purple = external.
218	
219	### 6.1 — F1 Produce: publish a record to a topic
220	
221	The producer must find the *leader* of the right partition, send the record, and only get an ack once the data is durable per its chosen `acks`.
222	
223	**Architecture decisions table**
224	
225	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
226	|---|---|---|---|---|
227	| Partitioner (client-side) | Logic in the producer that hashes the key to pick a partition | Keeps partition selection off the broker hot path; same key always lands together | Not server-side routing: adds a network hop and a broker bottleneck per write | Client must know partition count; a partition increase breaks key stickiness |
228	| Broker (partition leader) | A server process that owns the append-only log for its partitions | Only the leader writes, giving a single total order per partition for free | Not multi-writer per partition: would need consensus per record, killing throughput | Leader is a per-partition single point; failover needed (see 6.2) |
229	| Append-only segment log | The on-disk file the leader appends records to, split into segments | Sequential disk append is the fastest durable write pattern there is | Not a B-tree/row store: random writes are 10-100x slower and we never update in place | No in-place edits; deletes are whole-segment only |
230	| Metadata service | The cluster brain that tracks which broker leads which partition | Producers cache it and route directly; one source of truth for leadership | Not gossip-only: slower to converge, ambiguous leadership during churn | It's a global coordinator — must be HA (see §8) |
231	
232	> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
233	> **Partition leader** — the one broker that accepts writes for a partition. Here it gives us a single total order per partition without any cross-machine coordination per record.
234	> **Append-only segment log** — a file you only ever add to the end of, rolled into fixed-size segments. Here it makes writes a sequential disk append (very fast) and makes deletion a cheap whole-file drop.
235	> **Metadata service** — the cluster's directory of "which broker leads which partition." Here producers cache it so they send directly to the right leader with no extra hop.
236	> **acks** — the durability dial on a write: `1` = leader only, `all` = the whole in-sync set. Here it lets each producer trade latency for safety.
237	
238	**Diagram — Produce path (acks=all)**
239	
240	```mermaid
241	flowchart TD
242	    P[Producer app]
243	    P -->|"1 ask for leader"| Meta[(Metadata service)]
244	    P -->|"2 send record acks all"| Lead[Broker partition leader]
245	    subgraph Replication["Partition replica set"]
246	        Lead -->|"3 append to log"| Log[[Append only segment log]]
247	        Lead -->|"4 followers fetch"| F1b[Broker follower one]
248	        Lead -->|"4 followers fetch"| F2b[Broker follower two]
249	    end
250	    F1b -->|"5 ack to leader"| Lead
251	    F2b -->|"5 ack to leader"| Lead
252	    Lead -->|"6 ack with offset"| P
253	```
254	↳ reuses existing: Metadata service (introduced here), broker leader/follower (6.2 extends).
255	
256	**Numbered step narration**
257	1. **Producer asks for the leader** — it queries (or reads from cache) the metadata service for "who leads partition 7 of `orders.v1`." *Why metadata:* it's the single directory of leadership, so the producer routes directly with no broker-side proxy hop. *State change:* none yet. *What could go wrong:* metadata is stale after a failover → producer gets `NotLeader`, refreshes metadata, retries.
258	2. **Producer sends the record to the leader** with `acks=all`. *Why the leader:* only the leader writes, so this is where the single total order is assigned. *State change:* none committed yet. *Failure:* leader down mid-send → producer times out, refreshes metadata, retries (idempotent producer prevents a double-append).
259	3. **Leader appends to its log** and assigns the next offset. *State change:* record is on the leader's disk with offset 8042316, but NOT yet acknowledged. *Failure:* disk full → leader rejects, producer back-pressured.
260	4–5. **Followers fetch and ack** — the two ISR followers pull the record and confirm. *Why:* `acks=all` means we don't acknowledge until the ISR has it. *State change:* high-water mark advances to include 8042316. *Failure:* a follower lags → it drops out of the ISR; if ISR < `min.insync.replicas`, the write is refused (durability over availability).
261	6. **Leader acks the producer** with the assigned offset. *State change:* the write is now durable and visible to consumers (it's ≤ high-water mark). *Failure:* ack lost on the wire → producer retries → idempotent dedupe.
262	
263	> 💬 "The producer talks straight to the partition leader — and the acknowledgment doesn't come back until enough copies have it. That one rule is my entire durability guarantee."
264	
265	> 🎙️ **Script:** "A producer hashes the key to pick a partition, looks up that partition's leader, and sends the record. The leader appends it to an append-only log — sequential disk writes, the fastest durable pattern — and assigns an offset. With acks=all, it waits until the in-sync followers have copied the record before it acknowledges. That's the durability guarantee in one sentence: I don't say 'got it' until more than one machine has it. The trade-off is latency — waiting for replicas costs a few milliseconds — but for a system whose whole job is to not lose data, that's the right call."
266	
267	### 6.2 — F2 Replicate: leader/follower with ISR and failover
268	
269	This slice adds the *resilience* — what keeps a partition alive when its leader dies, without losing acknowledged data.
270	
271	**Architecture decisions table**
272	
273	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
274	|---|---|---|---|---|
275	| ISR (in-sync replica set) | The set of followers caught up to the leader within a lag bound | Durability rule: acked data lives on every ISR member, so any can take over losslessly | Not "all replicas must ack": one slow disk would stall every write | A lagging follower silently drops out, shrinking durability margin |
276	| Controller (leader elector) | The cluster role that detects a dead broker and elects a new partition leader from the ISR | Centralized election is fast and unambiguous; picks only from ISR so no data loss | Not per-partition Paxos: thousands of partitions × consensus = unmanageable | The controller is a global coordinator — needs its own HA (Raft quorum) |
277	| High-water mark | The highest offset all ISR members have; the read ceiling for consumers | Consumers never see un-replicated data, so a failover can't "un-send" a record they read | Not "read the leader's latest": a consumer could see data that vanishes on failover | Consumers read slightly behind the leader's tail |
278	
279	> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
280	> **ISR (in-sync replica set)** — the followers that are caught up enough to be trustworthy backups. Here it defines what "durable" means: acked data is on every ISR member.
281	> **Controller** — the elected cluster coordinator that re-assigns leadership when a broker dies. Here it picks the new leader only from the ISR, so failover never loses acknowledged data.
282	> **High-water mark** — the offset boundary that's safely replicated. Here it's the furthest a consumer is allowed to read, so a failover can never retract a record a consumer already saw.
283	
284	**Diagram — Leader failure and election**
285	
286	```mermaid
287	flowchart TD
288	    Ctrl[Controller raft quorum]
289	    Lead[Broker old leader]
290	    Ctrl -->|"1 detect leader dead"| Lead
291	    Ctrl -->|"2 pick new leader from ISR"| NewLead[Broker former follower now leader]
292	    subgraph Survivors["Surviving replica set"]
293	        NewLead -->|"3 serve writes"| Log[[Append only segment log]]
294	        NewLead -->|"4 followers refetch"| F2b[Broker other follower]
295	    end
296	    NewLead -->|"5 update metadata"| Meta[(Metadata service)]
297	    P[Producer app] -->|"6 refresh and resend"| NewLead
298	```
299	↳ reuses existing: Producer, Metadata service, segment log (from 6.1).
300	
301	**Numbered step narration**
302	1. **Controller detects the dead leader** via missed heartbeats. *Why controller:* one authority decides leadership, avoiding split-brain. *State:* the partition is briefly leaderless (writes pause). *Failure:* controller itself dies → its Raft quorum (a small odd-numbered group that votes) elects a new controller in seconds.
303	2. **Controller elects a new leader from the ISR** — never from an out-of-sync replica. *Why ISR-only:* an out-of-sync replica is missing acked data; promoting it would lose it. *State:* a former follower is now leader. *Failure:* ISR is empty (all replicas lagged) → either accept unclean election (data loss, configurable) or stay unavailable (we choose unavailable for money topics).
304	3–4. **New leader serves writes; remaining follower re-fetches** to re-sync. *State:* the partition is writable again, ISR rebuilds. *Failure:* the new leader also dies → repeat election.
305	5. **Metadata updated** so producers/consumers learn the new leader. *State:* leadership directory is current.
306	6. **Producer refreshes and resends** any in-flight record. *State:* idempotent producer ensures no double-append. *Failure:* none new — this is the recovery.
307	
308	> 💬 "Failover only ever promotes a replica that already has every acknowledged byte — that's why a crash can't lose data."
309	
310	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Each partition has a leader and a couple of followers, and the in-sync set is the followers that are caught up. When the leader dies, a controller — backed by its own small Raft voting group so it's never a single point — detects it and promotes a follower, but *only* one from the in-sync set, so the new leader already has every acknowledged byte. Consumers only ever read up to the high-water mark, the offset everyone has, so a failover can't retract something they already saw. The trade-off is that if the in-sync set shrinks to nothing, I'd rather refuse writes than promote a stale replica and lose data."
311	
312	### 6.3 — F3 Consume: consumer groups, offsets, and rebalancing
313	
314	This slice adds the read side and the hardest coordination problem: dividing partitions among a changing set of consumers without overlap or loss.
315	
316	**Architecture decisions table**
317	
318	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
319	|---|---|---|---|---|
320	| Group coordinator | A broker role that tracks group membership and owns the partition→consumer assignment | One authority per group prevents two consumers owning the same partition | Not client-side gossip: races and split assignments under churn | Coordinator failure triggers a rebalance |
321	| Committed offsets topic | An internal compacted topic storing each group's per-partition bookmark | Offsets are just durable log writes — no separate DB to operate | Not an external SQL/KV store: another system to scale, replicate, and page on | Compaction lag means offset reads are eventually consistent (fine — they're a floor) |
322	| Rebalance protocol | The handshake that reassigns partitions when membership changes | Cooperative/incremental version moves only the partitions that must move | Not stop-the-world eager rebalance: pauses the whole group on every change | Cooperative rebalance is more protocol rounds (more complex) |
323	
324	> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
325	> **Group coordinator** — the broker that manages one consumer group's membership and assignments. Here it guarantees no two consumers read the same partition at once.
326	> **Committed offsets topic** — an internal log (`__consumer_offsets`) that stores bookmarks. Here it means offsets are durable for free, with no extra database.
327	> **Rebalance** — the reassignment of partitions when a consumer joins or leaves. Here the cooperative variant moves only what must move, so most consumers keep reading.
328	> **Compacted topic** — a log that keeps only the latest value per key. Here it bounds the offsets topic's size to "one entry per group-partition."
329	
330	**Diagram — Consume + rebalance**
331	
332	```mermaid
333	flowchart TD
334	    C1[Consumer one in group]
335	    C2[Consumer two in group]
336	    Coord[Group coordinator]
337	    C1 -->|"1 join group"| Coord
338	    C2 -->|"1 join group"| Coord
339	    Coord -->|"2 assign partitions"| C1
340	    Coord -->|"2 assign partitions"| C2
341	    C1 -->|"3 fetch records"| Lead[Broker partition leader]
342	    C1 -->|"4 commit offset"| OffLog[(Committed offsets topic)]
343	    C3[Consumer three joins] -->|"5 trigger rebalance"| Coord
344	    Coord -->|"6 reassign subset"| C1
345	```
346	↳ reuses existing: partition leader (6.1).
347	
348	**Numbered step narration**
349	1. **Consumers join the group** by registering with the coordinator. *Why:* the coordinator needs the live membership to assign partitions. *State:* group membership = {C1, C2}. *Failure:* a consumer's heartbeat stops → coordinator evicts it and rebalances.
350	2. **Coordinator assigns partitions** (e.g. C1 ← partitions 0-3, C2 ← 4-7). *Why coordinator:* single authority = no overlap. *State:* assignment recorded. *Failure:* assignment message lost → consumer re-joins.
351	3. **Consumer fetches records** from each assigned partition's leader, forward from its committed offset. *State:* consumer position advances in memory. *Failure:* leader failover mid-fetch → consumer refreshes metadata, continues from committed offset.
352	4. **Consumer commits the offset** *after* processing, to the offsets topic. *State:* durable bookmark advances. *Failure:* crash before commit → on restart, re-reads from last commit → duplicate (at-least-once).
353	5–6. **A new consumer joins, triggering a rebalance** — the coordinator reassigns a *subset* of partitions to balance load (cooperative: C1 keeps 0-2, gives 3 to C3). *State:* new assignment; only the moved partition pauses briefly. *Failure:* a slow consumer doesn't ack the rebalance → coordinator times it out and proceeds.
354	
355	> 💬 "The coordinator is the referee — it guarantees no two consumers ever own the same partition, and the bookmark is just another durable log write."
356	
357	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Consumers in a group register with a coordinator, which splits the partitions among them so no two read the same one. Each consumer reads forward and commits its offset — its bookmark — *after* processing, and that commit is just a write to an internal compacted log, so I don't run a separate offsets database. When a consumer joins or dies, the coordinator rebalances; the cooperative version only moves the partitions that have to move, so the rest of the group keeps reading. The trade-off I accept is the duplicate window: if a consumer crashes after processing but before committing, it'll reprocess on restart — which is exactly why downstream consumers must be idempotent."
358	
359	### Final (high-level) — the reconciled union

366	    Ctrl[Controller raft quorum]

368	    OffLog[(Committed offsets topic)]

385	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Write path: a producer finds the leader via metadata, appends with acks=all, and the leader replicates to its in-sync followers before acknowledging. Resilience: a controller, on its own Raft quorum, elects a new leader from the in-sync set if one dies, so we never lose acknowledged data. Read path: consumers join a group, the coordinator hands each one a slice of partitions, they fetch forward and commit bookmarks to an internal offsets topic. The one event stream — the partition log — is shared by everyone; producers append, followers replicate, consumers replay."

391	## 7. Data Model & Storage
392	
393	### PART A — Entity table
394	
395	| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key annotated) | Chosen store | Shard / partition key | Consistency guarantee |
396	|---|---|---|---|---|
397	| Topic config | `topic_name` **(PK)**, partition_count, RF, retention_ms, cleanup_policy | Metadata store (Raft KV) | replicated to all | Strong (linearizable) |
398	| Partition log | `(topic, partition)` **(shard key)**, base_offset, records[] | Append-only segment files on broker NVMe | `(topic, partition)` → broker | Per-partition total order, RF=3 |
399	| Replica state | `(topic, partition, broker)` **(PK)**, log_end_offset, is_in_isr | Metadata store | `(topic, partition)` | Strong (controller-owned) |
400	| Consumer group | `group_id` **(PK)**, members[], assignment | Group coordinator (broker) | `group_id` → coordinator broker | Strong within coordinator |
401	| Committed offset | `(group, topic, partition)` **(PK)**, offset | `__consumer_offsets` compacted topic | hash(group) → partition | Read-your-commit (compacted) |
402	
403	### PART B — Storage decision cards
404	
405	**🗄️ Append-only segment log (on broker NVMe) — used for: the partition data itself**
406	1. **What it is:** a set of files on local disk that the broker only ever *appends* to, rolled into fixed-size segments named by their starting offset.
407	2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern:** the entire workload is "append at the tail, read sequentially forward, delete the oldest in bulk." Sequential append + the OS page cache makes both writes and reads hit memory/sequential-disk speeds, and zero-copy `sendfile` ships bytes from page cache straight to the network with no copy through user space.
408	3. **What we considered instead:**
409	   - **A relational DB (Postgres):** every record an INSERT with index maintenance — random I/O, MVCC bloat, and we'd hit a write wall orders of magnitude below 35 GB/s.
410	   - **A document/KV store (Cassandra/Dynamo):** great for point lookups, but we never look up by key — we stream by offset; their LSM compaction would fight our retention model and cost more.
411	   - **An object store (S3) as primary:** per-object latency and request cost make it wrong for the hot tail, though it's perfect for a *cold tier* (see lifecycle).
412	4. **Trade-off we accept:** no in-place updates and no secondary indexes — you can only read by offset, and deletion is whole-segment only.
413	5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** "The log is just append-only files on local NVMe. The workload is append-and-scan, never random lookup, so sequential I/O plus the OS page cache and zero-copy networking is the fastest thing there is. A database would die under random-write amplification at this rate. The cost is you can only read by offset and only delete whole segments — but that's exactly what a log needs."
414	
415	**🗄️ Metadata store (Raft-replicated KV) — used for: topic config, leadership, ISR state**
416	1. **What it is:** a small, strongly-consistent key-value store replicated by Raft (a voting protocol where a majority must agree before a change commits).
417	2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern:** cluster metadata (who leads what, who's in the ISR) must be *linearizable* — every broker must agree on the single current leader, or two brokers think they're leader and we corrupt the log. The data is tiny (KBs per partition) but the consistency requirement is absolute.
418	3. **What we considered instead:**
419	   - **ZooKeeper (external):** the classic choice, but it's a second system to operate, scale, and page on; the modern move (KRaft) folds it into the brokers.
420	   - **Gossip-based eventual consistency:** ambiguous leadership during churn → split-brain → data loss. Unacceptable for leadership.
421	4. **Trade-off we accept:** Raft writes need a majority round-trip, so metadata changes (leader election) take tens of milliseconds — fine, since they're rare.
422	5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** "Leadership and in-sync-set state live in a small Raft-replicated store, because everyone *must* agree on who the one leader is — if two brokers both think they lead a partition, I've corrupted the log. It's tiny data but it has to be linearizable. I fold it into the brokers rather than running a separate ZooKeeper to operate."
423	
424	**🗄️ `__consumer_offsets` (internal compacted topic) — used for: committed offsets**
425	1. **What it is:** an internal Kafka topic that stores `(group, topic, partition) → offset`, **compacted** so it keeps only the latest offset per key.
426	2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern:** offset commits are high-frequency writes that only ever need the *latest* value per key. A compacted log gives durability and bounded size for free, reusing the exact storage engine we already operate.
427	3. **What we considered instead:**
428	   - **External Redis/Postgres:** another stateful system to scale to millions of commits/s and replicate — pure operational drag.
429	   - **In-memory only:** loses all bookmarks on coordinator restart → mass reprocessing.
430	4. **Trade-off we accept:** compaction runs asynchronously, so the topic is briefly larger than its compacted floor; offset reads are a *floor* (you never read an offset higher than committed).
431	5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** "Offsets are just stored in another topic — a compacted one, so it keeps only the latest bookmark per group-partition. That means I get durability and reuse the same engine I already run, instead of bolting on a database that has to survive millions of commits a second."
432	
433	### PART C — Per-operation consistency
434	
435	| Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right |
436	|---|---|---|---|
437	| Produce `acks=all` | Partition log + ISR | Strong (RF-quorum durable) | Money/CDC topics cannot lose acked data |
438	| Produce `acks=1` | Partition log (leader) | Leader-only durable | Throughput topics (logs/metrics) tolerate rare loss |
439	| Leader election | Metadata store | Linearizable | Two leaders = log corruption |
440	| Consume read | Partition log | Read up to high-water mark | Never expose un-replicated data |
441	| Offset commit | `__consumer_offsets` | Read-your-commit | A consumer must resume from at least where it committed |
442	| Read-your-own-write (consumer) | Log | Same offset visible cluster-wide once ≤ high-water mark | After a produce ack, the offset is replicated, so any consumer on any replica sees it |
443	
444	**Read-your-own-writes trace:** a producer gets ack at offset 8042316 with `acks=all` → that offset is ≤ the high-water mark (the ISR has it) → therefore *any* consumer fetching that partition, from the leader or a follower, can read it. There's no stale-replica hole because consumers are capped at the high-water mark, which by definition is replicated.
445	
446	### PART D — Data lifecycle (the largest table: the partition log)
447	
448	At ~1 TB/day raw × RF 3, the partition log is ~27 PB at 7-day retention — that's the ~$2.2M/mo storage line and the reason we're storage-bound. **Reclamation is segment deletion, not row delete:** when a segment's newest record ages past `retention_ms`, the broker drops the *entire segment file* (O(1), instant) — never a per-record DELETE, which would churn indexes and never reclaim cleanly. **Cold tier:** segments older than the hot window (e.g. > 1 day) are offloaded to an object store (S3-class, ~$0.02/GB-mo, ~4× cheaper than NVMe) and read on demand for rare replays — this is the single biggest cost optimization, cutting hot storage roughly in half. **Compacted topics** (offsets, config) reclaim by keeping only the latest value per key instead of by time.
449	
450	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The data is the log — append-only segment files on local NVMe, because the workload is append-and-scan and sequential I/O with the OS page cache and zero-copy networking beats any database here. Cluster metadata — who leads what — lives in a small Raft store because everyone must agree on the one leader; I fold it into the brokers instead of running ZooKeeper. Offsets are stored in another compacted topic, so I reuse the same engine instead of operating a separate database. The big table is the log itself at twenty-seven petabytes; I reclaim it by dropping whole segment files when they age out, and I tier anything older than a day to object storage — that's my biggest cost win. I considered S3 as the *primary* store and rejected it: per-request latency is wrong for the hot tail, but it's perfect as the cold tier."
451

453	
454	## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great
455	
456	> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the two invariants — *acked data is never lost* and *per-partition order is never violated* — and reason forward from there.
457	
458	### How do we guarantee durability without killing throughput? (the ISR mechanism)
459	
460	**#### Bad: acks=1, leader-only**
461	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the leader writes the record to its own disk and immediately says "got it," without waiting for any backup.
462	- **Approach:** producer sends, leader appends, leader acks. Fast — one disk write, no replica round-trip.
463	- **Why people try this:** lowest possible latency and highest throughput; it *feels* durable because it's on disk.
464	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** the leader dies after acking but before any follower copied the record → that acknowledged write is **gone**. At ~35M writes/s, even a rare leader crash drops thousands of acked records — for a CDC topic that's permanent silent divergence from the source DB.
465	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** any topic where losing an acknowledged write is unacceptable (money, CDC) can't use `acks=1`.
466	
467	**#### Good: acks=all to ALL replicas**
468	- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** `acks=1` acked data only one machine had; this waits for *every* replica before acking, so a single death can't lose it.
469	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** don't say "got it" until all the backups have copied it too.
470	- **Approach:** leader appends, waits for *all* N replicas to confirm, then acks.
471	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** one slow follower (a GC pause, a bad disk) stalls *every* write to that partition — tail latency spikes and throughput collapses to the speed of the slowest replica. You've coupled availability to your unluckiest machine.
472	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** we need durability *without* one slow node holding everyone hostage.
473	
474	```mermaid
475	flowchart TD
476	    L[Leader appends record]
477	    L -->|"wait for ALL"| R1[Follower one]
478	    L -->|"wait for ALL"| R2[Follower two slow GC pause]
479	    R1 -->|"ack fast"| L
480	    R2 -->|"ack late blocks everyone"| L
481	    L -->|"ack only after slowest"| P[Producer waits]
482	```
483	
484	**#### Great: ISR + min.insync.replicas**
485	- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** waiting for *all* replicas let one slow node stall the partition; the ISR lets a lagging replica drop out so it no longer blocks writes, while still requiring a quorum to be durable.
486	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** keep a "currently caught-up" club; you only need the club members to confirm, and a slow member is quietly kicked out of the club so it can't slow you down — but if the club gets too small, you stop accepting writes rather than risk losing data.
487	- **Approach:** the leader maintains the **ISR** (followers within a lag bound). `acks=all` means "all *ISR* members," not all replicas. A follower that falls behind is removed from the ISR (stops blocking). `min.insync.replicas=2` means the leader refuses writes if the ISR drops below 2 — so an acked write always lives on ≥2 machines. On leader death, the controller elects only from the ISR → zero acked-data loss.
488	- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** RF=3, `min.insync=2`. We tolerate **1** replica failure with zero loss *and* zero write-stall (ISR still has 2). A 2nd simultaneous failure → ISR=1 < 2 → writes refused (unavailable, but no loss). So: 3 copies, 2 must ack, survive 1 failure transparently.
489	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
490	
491	| Scenario | What happens |
492	|---|---|
493	| 1 follower slow | drops out of ISR; writes continue on remaining ISR; no loss |
494	| 1 follower dies | ISR shrinks to 2; writes continue; failed replica re-syncs on return |
495	| Leader dies | controller promotes an ISR follower (has all acked data); no loss |
496	| ISR shrinks to 1 | leader refuses `acks=all` writes (min.insync=2); availability sacrificed, no loss |
497	| Network partition isolates leader | controller elects new leader on majority side; old leader steps down (can't reach ISR) |
498	
499	```mermaid
500	flowchart TD
501	    L[Leader maintains ISR]
502	    L -->|"in ISR"| R1[Follower one caught up]
503	    L -.->|"lagging removed from ISR"| R3[Follower three slow]
504	    R1 -->|"ack"| L
505	    L -->|"ack when ISR count meets min insync"| P[Producer]
506	    L -->|"if ISR below 2 refuse write"| Reject[Write rejected error]
507	```
508	
509	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The in-sync replica set is how I get durability without a slow node holding everyone hostage. The leader tracks which followers are caught up; an acknowledgment only needs the in-sync members, and a follower that lags gets quietly dropped from the set so it can't stall writes. The safety valve is min-insync-replicas — with three copies and a minimum of two, I survive any single failure with zero loss and zero stall, and if the set ever shrinks below two I refuse the write rather than acknowledge data only one machine has. Durability over availability for that write, deliberately."
510	
511	> 🧠 **If they ask "why not just acks=all to all replicas?":** "Because one GC pause on one follower would stall every write to that partition. The ISR decouples durability from the slowest machine — I still need a quorum, but a lagging node leaves the quorum instead of blocking it."
512	
513	### How do we rebalance consumers without losing or double-reading? (offset + rebalance)
514	
515	**#### Bad: static assignment, commit-then-process**
516	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** hard-code which consumer reads which partition, and bookmark the page *before* you've actually read it.
517	- **Approach:** fixed partition→consumer map; consumer commits offset, then processes.
518	- **Why people try this:** simple, no coordinator, and committing first "feels" safe.
519	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** (a) a dead consumer's partitions go *unread* forever — no failover. (b) Commit-then-process *loses* messages: commit offset 100, crash before processing 100 → on restart you resume at 101, and 100 is silently skipped. That's **at-most-once** — data loss.
520	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** we need automatic reassignment on failure AND a commit order that doesn't drop messages.
521	
522	**#### Good: group coordinator + eager rebalance + commit-after-process**
523	- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** static assignment had no failover and commit-first lost data; a coordinator adds automatic reassignment and committing *after* processing flips us to at-least-once (duplicates, never loss).
524	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** a referee reassigns chapters when readers come and go, and you only bookmark a page *after* you've actually read it.
525	- **Approach:** the **group coordinator** tracks membership via heartbeats; on any change it triggers a **stop-the-world rebalance** — every consumer drops its partitions, the coordinator recomputes the full assignment, everyone resumes from committed offsets. Consumers commit *after* processing.
526	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** the stop-the-world rebalance pauses the *entire group* on every join/leave — in an autoscaling fleet of hundreds of consumers, scaling events cause repeated full pauses ("rebalance storms"), and a slow rejoin stalls everyone. Consumer lag spikes on every deploy.
527	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** we need rebalances that don't pause the whole group.
528	
529	```mermaid
530	flowchart TD
531	    Coord[Group coordinator]
532	    Coord -->|"member change"| Stop[All consumers stop and revoke]
533	    Stop -->|"recompute full assignment"| Assign[Coordinator reassigns everything]
534	    Assign -->|"resume from committed offsets"| Run[All consumers resume]
535	    Run -.->|"any join or leave repeats"| Coord
536	```
537	
538	**#### Great: cooperative incremental rebalance + idempotent consumers**
539	- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** eager rebalance stopped the whole group; cooperative rebalance moves only the partitions that must change owner, so most consumers never pause.
540	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** when one reader joins, only hand over the few chapters that need a new owner — everyone else keeps reading without interruption.
541	- **Approach:** **cooperative (incremental) rebalance** runs in two phases — the coordinator computes the *diff*, only the consumers losing a partition revoke it, then those partitions are assigned to the new owner. Consumers keep their unaffected partitions throughout. Combined with **commit-after-process** (at-least-once) and **idempotent consumers** (dedupe by a natural key, e.g. `order_id`, in a dedupe store with a TTL), the unavoidable duplicate window on crash becomes harmless.
542	- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** eager rebalance on a 200-consumer fleet pauses all 200 on every scale event; cooperative pauses only the ~1-2 consumers whose partitions actually move — a ~100× reduction in disrupted consumers per event, turning multi-second group-wide stalls into sub-second localized ones.
543	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
544	
545	| Scenario | What happens |
546	|---|---|
547	| Consumer dies | coordinator times out its heartbeat, reassigns only its partitions to others |
548	| Consumer crashes after process, before commit | on restart, re-reads from last commit → duplicate → idempotent consumer dedupes |
549	| Coordinator dies | a new coordinator is elected (it's a partition leader of the offsets topic); group re-discovers it |
550	| Slow consumer | coordinator evicts it after max-poll timeout; its partitions move; it rejoins later |
551	| Duplicate delivery | dedupe store keyed by natural key rejects the second apply; effective exactly-once *processing* |
552	
553	```mermaid
554	flowchart TD
555	    Coord[Group coordinator]
556	    NewC[New consumer joins]
557	    NewC -->|"1 join"| Coord
558	    Coord -->|"2 revoke only moved partition"| C1[Consumer one keeps rest]
559	    Coord -->|"3 assign moved partition"| NewC
560	    C1 -->|"4 unaffected partitions keep reading"| Lead[Partition leader]
561	    NewC -->|"5 process then commit"| OffLog[(Committed offsets topic)]
562	    NewC -.->|"6 dedupe by natural key"| Dedupe[(Dedupe store TTL)]
563	```
564	
565	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The naive version statically assigns partitions and bookmarks before reading — that has no failover and actually loses data. So I use a coordinator that reassigns on failure, and I commit *after* processing, which makes it at-least-once: a crash before commit means a re-read, never a loss. The upgrade is cooperative rebalancing — when a consumer joins, I only move the one or two partitions that have to change hands, so the other hundred-and-ninety-eight consumers never pause. The leftover duplicate window I close at the consumer with idempotent processing — dedupe by a natural key — so reprocessing a message is a no-op. That's effectively exactly-once *processing* on top of at-least-once *delivery*."
566	
567	> 🧠 **If they ask "why not just do exactly-once delivery?":** "True exactly-once needs transactional producers and a transaction coordinator across produce-process-produce — real throughput and latency cost. For most topics, at-least-once delivery plus an idempotent consumer is cheaper and gives the same business outcome."
568	
569	### How do we stop a slow consumer from taking down a fast producer? (back-pressure)
570	
571	**#### Bad: unbounded broker-side buffering**
572	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** if a reader is slow, just let unread data pile up forever and hope it catches up.
573	- **Approach:** brokers retain everything; slow consumers just have high lag.
574	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** lag is *fine* up to retention — but a consumer stalled longer than `retention_ms` has its un-read data **deleted out from under it** (data loss for that consumer), and a producer pushing faster than disk can absorb fills the page cache and stalls *all* consumers. Unbounded anything is a latent outage.
575	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** we need an explicit signal and a bounded response.
576	
577	**#### Good: consumer-paced pull + lag monitoring**
578	- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** unbounded buffering hid the problem until retention deleted data; a *pull* model plus lag alarms makes slowness visible and self-limiting.
579	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** consumers ask for data at their own pace instead of being firehosed, and we alarm when any reader falls too far behind.
580	- **Approach:** consumption is **pull-based** — a slow consumer simply fetches less often, so it can never be overwhelmed; the broker isn't pushing. We alarm on **consumer lag** (high-water mark minus committed offset) per group.
581	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** pull protects the *consumer*, but a *producer* outrunning total disk/network throughput still has nowhere for its data to go — and lag alarms are reactive, firing after the consumer is already hours behind, with retention as a hard deadline.
582	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** we need to back-pressure the *producer* and shed load *before* retention bites.
583	
584	```mermaid
585	flowchart TD
586	    Prod[Producer pushes fast]
587	    Prod -->|"append"| Log[[Partition log retention bound]]
588	    Slow[Slow consumer pulls at own pace] -->|"fetch when ready"| Log
589	    Log -.->|"lag alarm fires"| Alert[On call paged]
590	```
591	
592	**#### Great: bounded queues + producer quotas + tiered load-shedding**
593	- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** pull protected consumers but not the system; quotas and bounded buffers push back on the *producer* and shed in a deliberate order before retention deletes data.
594	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** cap how fast each producer can push, make its send buffer finite so it blocks (or drops) when full, and if the whole cluster is overloaded, throttle the least-important traffic first to protect the critical topics.
595	- **Approach:** (1) **producer quotas** — per-client byte-rate limits; over-quota producers get throttled (the broker delays their responses, applying back-pressure up the stack). (2) **Bounded producer buffer** — the client's send buffer is finite; when full it either *blocks* the calling thread or *drops* with an error (configurable) — never unbounded memory growth. (3) **Tiered shedding under cluster overload** — shed/throttle low-priority topics (debug logs, metrics) first; protect the money/CDC topics last. **Trigger signal:** broker request-queue depth + per-partition produce latency p99 breach. (4) **Tiered storage** extends the retention deadline cheaply so a lagging consumer has hours more runway.
596	- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** a hot topic at 100 MB/s with 7-day retention buys a stalled consumer **~168 hours** of runway before data is deleted — but only if producers are quota-capped so they can't fill the disk faster than retention frees it. Without quotas, a runaway producer at 3× burst exhausts that buffer in ~56 hours.
597	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
598	
599	| Scenario | What happens |
600	|---|---|
601	| One consumer stalls | its lag grows; alarm fires; it has ~168h of retention runway; others unaffected (pull) |
602	| Producer exceeds quota | broker throttles its responses; producer's bounded buffer back-pressures the app |
603	| Producer buffer full | blocks the app thread (or drops with error) — never unbounded memory |
604	| Cluster-wide overload | shed debug/metrics topics first; protect money/CDC; trigger on queue depth + p99 |
605	| Consumer lag past retention | un-read data deleted for that consumer (accepted loss for low-tier topics; alarmed early for high-tier) |
606	
607	```mermaid
608	flowchart TD
609	    Prod[Producer over quota]
610	    Prod -->|"1 send"| QChk[Broker quota check]
611	    QChk -->|"2 throttle response"| Prod
612	    QChk -->|"3 within quota append"| Log[[Partition log]]
613	    Buf[Producer bounded buffer] -.->|"4 block app when full"| App[Producer app thread]
614	    Overload[Cluster overload signal] -->|"5 shed low priority topics first"| Shed[Throttle debug and metrics]
615	    Log -.->|"6 tier old segments"| Cold[(Object store cold tier)]
616	```
617	
618	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Slow consumers can't hurt producers directly because consumption is pull-based — a slow reader just fetches less, the broker never firehoses it. The real risk is a producer outrunning the cluster, so I cap each producer with a byte-rate quota and give it a *bounded* send buffer that blocks or drops when full — never unbounded memory, which is a latent outage. Under cluster-wide overload I shed in priority order: throttle debug and metrics topics first, protect money and CDC last, triggered on broker queue depth and produce-latency p99. And tiered storage buys a lagging consumer extra hours of runway by pushing old segments to cheap object storage. The principle is: every buffer is bounded, and I always protect the correctness path."
619	
620	> 🧠 **If they ask "what's the trigger to shed load?":** "Broker request-queue depth crossing a threshold plus a produce-latency p99 breach — both leading indicators, so I shed before consumers fall past retention, not after."
621
